title: 41st International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2025 — Junior (Grades 9-10)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

solutions.1: When the two grey parts of the sheet are folded over the fold lines, the transparent windows reveal numbers on the parts beneath. Tracking which numbers appear in each window after each fold and summing them gives the total. The visual folding logic leads to the sum corresponding to answer D.
solutions.2: If the base of a triangle increases by a factor of 3/2 (increases by half) and the height decreases by one-third (factor of 2/3), the new area = (3/2)*(2/3) of the original = 1. The ratio of the new area to the original area equals 1, corresponding to answer B.
solutions.3: For a regular hexagon to have exactly 1/3 of its area black and 1/2 white, we need 2 of its 6 equal triangular sections black and 3 white (with 1 in another color). Among the five options, hexagon E satisfies both constraints simultaneously. Answer E.
solutions.4: Kangaroo Day is the third Thursday of March. The earliest possible date occurs when March 1st is a Thursday: then the Thursdays fall on March 1, 8, and 15. The third Thursday is March 15th. For any other starting day, the third Thursday falls on March 16 or later. Earliest possible date is March 15th, answer B.
solutions.5: The recipe ratio is cups of rice to cups of water. To scale the recipe from the original amount of rice to 2 cups, multiply the water proportionally. Using the given ratio and scaling factor, Ahmed needs the number of cups of water corresponding to answer D.
solutions.6: Laila has four wooden digits and forms numbers by rearranging them. The count of different numbers greater than the given number is determined by listing all permutations of those digits and counting those exceeding the threshold. The total is answer C.
solutions.7: Fold a square along its diagonal to form a right isosceles triangle (angles 90, 45, 45 degrees). The hypotenuse is the long edge and the two equal sides are the short edges. Fold again so one short edge lies along the hypotenuse: the fold line bisects the 45-degree angle at one corner, creating a 22.5-degree angle. The angle in the smaller triangle asked about is answer B.
solutions.8: The multi-digit number is missing its last two digits and must be divisible by given divisors. Using divisibility rules for those divisors, the last two missing digits are uniquely determined. Their product equals answer D.
solutions.9: Let the number of dogs = d, rabbits = r, cats = c. Not dogs = 8 means r+c=8. Not rabbits = 5 means d+c=5. Not cats = 7 means d+r=7. Adding all three: 2(d+r+c) = 20, so total pets = 10. Answer A.
solutions.10: Circle has center O and radius 10 cm. Square OPQR has Q on the circle, so OQ = 10 cm = diagonal of the square. Side length = 10/sqrt(2) = 5*sqrt(2). Triangle PQR is formed by three consecutive vertices of the square: it is a right isosceles triangle with legs PQ = QR = 5*sqrt(2). Area = (1/2)*(5*sqrt(2))^2 = (1/2)*50 = 25 cm^2. Answer B.
solutions.11: An athlete has 2 gold and 5 silver medals numbered 1–7. In each photo exactly one medal is gold. By examining all photos and applying the constraint (each photo shows exactly one gold), we systematically eliminate medals that must be silver: if a medal appears as the non-gold item in one photo and the gold item in another, contradictions arise. The two gold medals are identified by elimination, and their numbers sum to the value given by answer C. (The exact pair depends on the specific photo layout shown in the competition paper.)
solutions.12: The smartphone display has aspect ratio 16:9. In landscape orientation the photo fills the screen exactly. When rotated to portrait (9:16 display), the 16:9 photo must fit inside without cropping. The limiting constraint is the width: the photo width scales down to match the portrait width of 9 units, giving photo width = 9 and photo height = 9 × (9/16) = 81/16. Photo area = 9 × 81/16 = 729/16; display area = 9 × 16 = 144. Fraction = (729/16) ÷ 144 = 729/2304 = 81/256.
solutions.13: Let K = Khawlah's age and A = Ahmed's age. The condition (1/19)K = (1/17)A gives 17K = 19A, so K = 19A/17. For K to be a whole number, A must be a multiple of 17. A = 17 gives K = 19 and sum = 36 (too small, < 40). A = 34 gives K = 38 and sum = 72, which is between 40 and 100. ✓ A = 51 gives sum = 108 (too large, > 100). Therefore Khawlah is 38 years old.
solutions.14: Let L = shots aimed at the top-left target and R = shots at the bottom-right. Then L + R = 27. Misses: 0.5L (top-left) + 0.2R (bottom-right) = 9. Substituting R = 27 − L: 0.5L + 0.2(27 − L) = 9 → 0.3L = 3.6 → L = 12. Hits on the top-left target = 50% × 12 = 6.
solutions.15: Primes from 1 to 18: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17 — that is 7 prime-numbered balls. Non-primes (including 1): 1, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18 — that is 11 balls. Worst case: Sara removes all 11 non-prime balls first, then 2 prime balls (13 total) — still only 2 primes removed. On the 14th removal, she must pick a prime-numbered ball, guaranteeing at least 3 primes. So the minimum number to guarantee 3 prime balls is 14.
solutions.16: The diagram arranges 8 cells in a specific connected shape. The constraint is that no two consecutive integers (e.g. 3 and 4) may share a side or vertex. Cell X sits in a position with many neighbors, so any number placed there must not be consecutive with any of its adjacent cells' numbers. Through systematic case analysis of the arrangement, only 2 and 7 can be placed in cell X: 2 has only 1 and 3 as consecutive neighbors, and 7 has only 6 and 8 — both can avoid adjacency conflicts given the geometry. All other digits create unavoidable consecutive-adjacency. So the answer is 2 or 7.
solutions.17: We need the largest 6-digit integer N whose digit product equals 180 = 4 × 9 × 5 = 2² × 3² × 5. To maximise N, we want the leading digits as large as possible. Decompose 180 into six single digits (1–9) with the leading digits as large as possible: 9 × 5 × 4 × 1 × 1 × 1 = 180, giving digits {9, 5, 4, 1, 1, 1} and the largest arrangement 954111. Any decomposition using smaller leading digits (e.g. 6, 6, 5 or 9, 5, 2, 2) gives a smaller number. Sum of digits of N = 9 + 5 + 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 21.
solutions.18: Let each congruent shaded rectangle have dimensions a × b with area ab = 4. From the figure, one rectangle is placed in one corner of ABCD and the other in the opposite corner, with a specific overlapping or tiling arrangement. Setting up the constraint equations from the given configuration and the congruency condition determines the dimensions of ABCD. The area of rectangle ABCD equals the value given by answer D.
solutions.19: Let p, q, r be prime numbers with pqr = 11(p + q + r). We seek the largest possible sum S = p + q + r. Try p = 11: 11qr = 11(11 + q + r) → qr − q − r = 11 → (q−1)(r−1) = 12. Factoring 12: (q−1, r−1) = (1, 12) gives q = 2, r = 13 (both prime) → S = 11+2+13 = 26. (q−1, r−1) = (2, 6) gives q = 3, r = 7 → S = 21 (smaller). Try p = 2: (2q−11)(2r−11) = 165. Factoring: (11,15) → q = 11, r = 13 → S = 26 (same). No other prime triples give a larger sum. The largest possible value of the sum is 26.
solutions.20: The five bricks form a 3-2 pyramid: bricks 1, 2, 3 on the bottom, bricks 4 (on 1,2) and 5 (on 2,3) on top. Initially only bricks 4 and 5 are accessible. For brick 4 to be the 3rd removed: first remove brick 5 (prob 1/2), then remove brick 3 (newly accessible, chosen from {4, 3} with prob 1/2), then brick 4 is the only one left accessible. P(brick 4 is 3rd) = (1/2) × (1/2) = 1/4.
solutions.21: Square ABCD contains a grey rectangle (7 × 5 cm, placed at the top-left corner) and a striped rectangle (8 × 7 cm, placed at the bottom-right corner). Their overlap dimensions are: horizontal = 7 + 8 − s and vertical = 5 + 7 − s, where s is the side length of the square. Setting overlap area = 18: (15 − s)(12 − s) = 18 → s² − 27s + 162 = 0. Solving: s = (27 ± 9)/2, giving s = 9 (the valid solution since 18 and 13 would be too large). Perimeter = 4 × 9 = 36 cm.
solutions.22: ABCD × D = DXYA means the units digit of ABCD² ≡ A (mod 10), so A = D² mod 10. For the result to remain a 4-digit number and have thousands digit D, we need ABCD × D < 10000 and ≥ 1000D. Testing D = 9: A = 81 mod 10 = 1, so ABCD = 1BC9 and 1BC9 × 9 ≤ 9999 → 1BC9 ≤ 1111. Valid numbers: 1BC9 from 1009 to 1111, i.e. B = 0 (any C: 10 values 1009–1099) or B = 1, C = 0 (one value: 1109). All other values of D (1–8) either produce 5-digit results or fail the thousands-digit condition. Total count = 10 + 1 = 11 four-digit integers.
solutions.23: The spiral numbers squares counter-clockwise starting from 1. Key observation: 2025 = 45², and a counter-clockwise spiral that starts at the centre fills a (2k+1) × (2k+1) square exactly after (2k+1)² steps. With 2025 = 45² = (2×22+1)², the 2025 squares form a perfect 45 × 45 grid. Each small square has side 0.5 cm, so the overall shape is 45 × 0.5 = 22.5 cm per side. Perimeter = 4 × 22.5 = 90 cm.
solutions.24: ABCDE must be divisible by 5, so E = 5 (the only digit 1–6 ending in 0 or 5). AB must be divisible by 2, so B ∈ {2, 4, 6}. Using divisibility by 4 (CD divisible by 4) and divisibility by 3 (digit sum divisibility), with remaining digits {1,2,3,4,6} for A,B,C,D,F: Testing B = 2, F = 4: valid arrangements include 321654 and 123654, both satisfying all conditions. In every valid solution F = 4. (Total digit sum = 21 is already divisible by 3, ensuring divisibility by 6 automatically.)
solutions.25: Let the outer circle have radius R and the inner white circle have radius r, with the inner circle's diameter lying along the outer circle's diameter. The chord of length 16 is parallel to the common diameter and tangent to the inner circle, so the perpendicular distance from the inner centre to the chord equals r. By the chord-distance formula: R² − r² = (16/2)² = 64. Shaded area = π R² − π r² = π(R² − r²) = 64π.
solutions.26: For n ≥ 3, aₙ = mean of all previous terms, so a₁+…+aₙ₋₁ = (n−1)aₙ. Then a₁+…+aₙ = naₙ, and aₙ₊₁ = (a₁+…+aₙ)/n = aₙ. So every term from a₃ onwards is equal: a₃ = a₄ = … = a₁₀ = 26. From a₃ = (a₁ + a₂)/2: 26 = (8 + a₂)/2 → a₂ = 52 − 8 = 44.
solutions.27: Six circles form a triangle: 3 at vertices (values a, b, c) and 3 on the edges (d, e, f). Each side sums to S = a+d+b = b+e+c = a+f+c. Adding all three sides: (a+b+c) + 21 = 3S, so vertex sum V = a+b+c = 3S−21. V must be divisible by 3. With values 1–6, V ranges from 6 to 15, giving V ∈ {6, 9, 12, 15}. Each case admits a valid arrangement: V=6 → S=9: (1,2,3) at vertices, (6,5,4) on edges; V=9 → S=10: (1,3,5) at vertices, (6,2,4) on edges; V=12 → S=11: (2,4,6) at vertices, (5,1,3) on edges; V=15 → S=12: (4,5,6) at vertices, (3,1,2) on edges. There are 4 possible values for the vertex sum.
solutions.28: The 3 twin pairs each get the same hat colour: either both blue or both red. If k twin pairs get blue hats (k = 0,1,2,3), they use 2k blue hats. The remaining 6−2k blue hats go to non-twin children (6 of them). Number of ways = Σ C(3,k) × C(6, 6−2k): k=0: 1×C(6,6)=1; k=1: 3×C(6,4)=45; k=2: 3×C(6,2)=45; k=3: 1×C(6,0)=1. Total = 1+45+45+1 = 92.
solutions.29: Place A=(0,0), B=(10,0), C=(0,12) so area of ABC = 60. I = midpoint of BC = (5,6). J divides AC such that AJ = AC/3, so J=(0,4). Line AI: parametric (5t, 6t). Line BJ: parametric (10−10s, 4s). Intersection L: 5t=10−10s and 6t=4s → s=3t/2 → 5t=10−15t → t=1/2. So L=(5/2, 3). Area of triangle ALJ with A=(0,0), L=(5/2,3), J=(0,4): = (1/2)|det[(5/2, 3),(0, 4)]| = (1/2)|5/2×4 − 3×0| = (1/2)(10) = 5.
solutions.30: This counts 2×4 Standard Young Tableaux (SYT): fill a 2×4 grid with 1–8 such that each row increases left-to-right and each column increases top-to-bottom. By the hook-length formula: number of SYT = 8! ÷ (product of hook lengths). Hook lengths for 2×4: row 1 = 5,4,3,2; row 2 = 4,3,2,1. Product = 5×4×3×2×4×3×2×1 = 120×24 = 2880. Number of ways = 40320 ÷ 2880 = 14.
